text: The surgeon begins by taking down the medial attachments of the gallbladder from the dome down to the Triangle of Calot, exposing the medial side of the gallbladder and the Triangle of Calot. This technique enhances visibility compared to retracting the fundus and directly approaching the cystic duct and artery. The surgeon continues to detach the gallbladder from the liver bed, moving from medial to lateral, to improve exposure and avoid working in a confined space.

The surgeon uses a My clip-up plier as a retractor to elevate the liver, enhancing exposure of the Triangle of Calot. The medial attachments of the gallbladder are dissected from the dome down to the Triangle of Calot, revealing the cystic duct connecting to the common hepatic duct. The Firefly imaging system aids in visualizing the anatomy, providing clear exposure despite the presence of a fatty liver and surrounding tissues.

The surgeon uses a My clip-up plier as a retractor to elevate the liver, ensuring clear exposure of the surgical field. The Firefly imaging system enhances visualization, showing the cystic duct connecting to the common hepatic duct. The gallbladder fundus and cystic duct are retracted away from the liver, providing a safe distance from the right hepatic artery and duct. The surgeon prepares to take the lateral attachments of the gallbladder before proceeding to skeletonize the cystic duct and artery.

The surgeon pulls the gallbladder fundus and cystic duct away from the liver, creating space to work safely away from critical structures like the right hepatic artery and duct. The lateral attachments of the gallbladder are being taken down to facilitate further dissection.

The surgeon uses wristed robotic instruments to perform a precise dissection, pulling the body of the gallbladder away to separate the cystic duct from the surrounding structures in the cystic triangle. The gallbladder is progressively detached from its attachments.

The robotic instruments are used to manipulate the gallbladder, pulling it away from the liver to expose the cystic duct and cystic artery. The surgeon carefully dissects the gallbladder towards the neck, utilizing Firefly imaging to ensure no aberrant ducts are present, while maintaining a safe distance from critical structures.

The surgeon uses Firefly imaging to confirm the absence of aberrant ducts joining the cystic duct while dissecting towards the neck of the gallbladder. The cystic artery is identified, and the cystic duct is carefully skeletonized, achieving the critical view of safety with only two structures visible entering the gallbladder. The entire gallbladder is separated from the liver, ensuring no other structures are connected to it.

The surgeon identifies the cystic artery and uses the Firefly imaging system to ensure no other ducts are joining the cystic duct. The critical view of safety is achieved, showing only two structures entering the gallbladder. The entire gallbladder is separated from the liver, ensuring no other structures are connected to it.

The surgeon uses a clip applier to secure a clip on the cystic artery, followed by division of the artery using cautery. The setup involves four ports and three instruments, providing efficient exposure without the need for additional tools like scissors.

The surgeon efficiently clips the cystic artery using a clip applier, then divides it with cautery. Three clips are placed on the cystic duct, with two on the patient's side, allowing for cautery on the specimen side. The gallbladder is removed through the umbilical defect using an endocatch bag.

The surgeon places three clips on the cystic duct, with two clips positioned on the patient's side. Cautery is applied on the specimen side to divide the duct. An endocatch bag is used to retrieve the gallbladder through the umbilical defect, completing the procedure.
